fed_monetary: The FOMC held the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75% for a second consecutive meeting on March 18 in an 11-1 vote, with the March dot plot still projecting only a single cut in 2026 even as the Committee revised its core PCE forecast higher to 2.7%; the median long-run rate edged up to 3.125%, signaling an incrementally less accommodative neutral. With core PCE running at 2.97% YoY, sticky CPI at 2.9% (Atlanta Fed), and PPI printing 3.22% against a 2.60% consensus, the Fed's data-dependent pause is firmly intact ahead of the April 29 decision, where futures markets currently assign near-zero probability to a cut. The balance sheet (WALCL $6.68T) continues passive runoff, leaving composite liquidity at just the 33rd percentile — a meaningful but not acute drag on risk assets.
labor_data: March nonfarm payrolls surged +178K against a +100K consensus, driven heavily by health care (+76K), though the unemployment rate's dip to 4.3% was partly a statistical artifact of a sharp labor force contraction. Wage growth decelerated meaningfully — average hourly earnings rose just 0.2% MoM and 3.5% YoY, the softest annualized print since May 2021 — tempering any second-round inflation concern from the labor channel. Initial claims of 219K (vs. 210K estimate) are consistent with modest incremental softening at the margin, but the labor market broadly remains resilient enough to preclude any near-term dovish pivot.
fiscal_supply: The FY2026 cumulative deficit reached $919 billion through February — the third-largest four-month deficit in six years — with net interest costs and defense spending as the primary spending accelerants, partially offset by a $19B surge in customs duties receipts attributable to tariff pass-through. Treasury's quarterly refunding guidance anticipates $683 billion in borrowing across Q1–Q2 FY2026 (January–June), with the TGA projected to peak near $1.025T by late April before declining in May; at $847.7B currently, the TGA build is absorbing reserves ($3.03T) but the near-zero RRP balance ($0.2B) leaves little excess liquidity buffer if supply pressures intensify. Debt held by the public is approaching its post-WWII high as a percentage of GDP, a structural premium risk that keeps term premia elevated.
corporate: HY OAS stands at 294 bps and IG OAS at 105 bps — both tight by historical standards but subject to creep. Nomura's April update notes global HY spreads widened only ~30 bps in March to 343 bps, with US HY absorbing the Treasury sell-off with a muted 16 bps of widening, supported by record-high aggregate credit quality (59% BB-rated) and strong operating earnings. Tariff headwinds remain a tangible EPS risk — P&G's CFO has flagged a 5-point drag to core EPS growth in FY2026 — and energy/A&D sectors are net beneficiaries of elevated WTI ($97.63), while airlines, consumer discretionary, and housing-related issuers face margin pressure. Risk appetite is constructive (VIX 20.09, SPX +0.59%) but the signal distribution skews 3-hawk/3-tight vs. zero dovish readings, suggesting the upside for credit is asymmetrically capped.
next_catalyst: The March CPI print — scheduled for release Friday, April 10 at 8:30 a.m. ET — is the single most consequential near-term data point: with February CPI already running hot at 2.66% YoY (vs. 2.4% estimate) and tariff pass-through accelerating import costs, an upside surprise would materially compress the already-thin window for the Fed's sole projected 2026 cut and could reprice the April 29 FOMC meeting from a firm hold to a hawkish-hold with revised forward guidance.
